summary: DHH draws a parallel between the historical European enclosure movement—where public commons were privatized by lords—and Big Tech's enclosure of the open internet. He argues that the period from roughly 1995-2010 represented a brief commons era where software flowed freely from makers to customers. The rise of smartphones, mislabeled as 'phones' rather than pocket computers, gave Apple and Google the rhetorical cover to impose app store monopolies and 30% taxes on software distribution. This forced developers into unsavory business models like ads and tracking. He frames the fight against app store monopolies as part of a broader struggle to preserve open internet commons, urging people to choose open platforms over enclosed ones.
key_points: The open internet era of 1995-2010 was a brief commons period, analogous to the post-plague era of European commoner prosperity before enclosure
Calling smartphones 'phones' instead of pocket computers gave Apple rhetorical cover to compare app distribution to the old feature phone model rather than the open Mac software market of 2005
Apple's 30% app store tax parallels how lords enclosed formerly free commons, squeezing developers into running faster, capping growth, or pivoting to ad-driven models
Facebook, Apple, and Google fight not over whether to enclose the internet but over who gets to do it and who gets the spoils
The antidote is choosing open platforms—newsletters over Twitter, podcasts over Facebook, direct distribution over app stores
money_quotes: This glorious phase of freedom unfortunately did not even last the 150 years that Hickel examined between the plague and the capitalist acceleration of the European enclosure movement, but rather a brief 15 or so.

That's the time Big Tech companies like Apple would like you to forget about. Those glorious fifteen years where most software flowed freely and unencumbered across an unenclosed internet, directly from makers to customers.

This misnomer, that the computer I carry in my pocket should be summarized by the feature most of us use the least, has allowed Apple the rhetorical opening to compare today's mobile software distribution to that of feature phones.

Apple and Google are simply acting like the lords that in the early capitalist era decided that what used to be free or at least cheap — distributing software over the internet — now suddenly needed to be taxed at 30%.
This naturally caused the commoners, app developers, to become squeezed in the process. To either run a third faster, cap the capacity to grown by a third, or turn to other unsavory business models.

Facebook and Apple and Google might bicker amongst themselves from time to time, just like I'm sure the lords of the enclosure movement did back in the 1600s and 1700s, but in their essence, they're more alike than they are different.
Fighting not over whether or not to enclose the internet commons, but about who gets to do it, and what portion of the spoils should go to which set of shareholders.
But it's part of the same struggle that wins when people dump Twitter for a newsletter or Facebook for a podcast. Open pastures on internet commons, not enclosed or captured by any one company.
key_insight: Big Tech's app store monopolies and platform dominance are a modern enclosure movement, privatizing what was once an open internet commons and taxing developers who formerly distributed software freely.
